question: A patient being evaluated for language dysfunction undergoes diffusion tensor tractography as part of preoperative planning. Based on the imaging, which symptom would be most likely?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image: a diffusion tensor tractography reconstruction with colored streamlines overlaid on a sagittal MRI. The prominent bundle of fibers courses from the posterior temporal region upward and forward toward the inferior frontal region, forming a curving arcuate-like tract between posterior and anterior language-related areas.

Next, I relate what I see to the question, which asks which language symptom would be most likely given this imaging. The image emphasizes an intact (or depicted) connecting white-matter pathway running between posterior temporal and frontal language zones rather than showing an isolated cortical lesion.

Then, I consider how each option aligns with that observation. Option A (impaired verbal repetition) directly implicates disruption of the connection between posterior temporal (comprehension) and frontal (production) areas; damage to the tract visible here would plausibly produce a repetition deficit. Option B (nonfluent/expressive aphasia) and option C (fluent/receptive aphasia with poor comprehension) reflect primarily focal cortical dysfunction of frontal or temporal cortex, respectively, which is not specifically depicted by tractography of the connecting fibers. Option D (right hemispatial neglect) refers to a visuospatial/parietal phenomenon unrelated to the language fiber bundle shown.

Finally, tying the observations to the choices: because the image highlights the major fiber pathway linking posterior temporal and frontal language regions, a symptom reflecting interruption of that connection—impaired verbal repetition—is the most consistent finding.
caption: Visualization of the arcuate fasciculus by DTI.
sub_caption: Visualization of the arcuate fasciculus by DTI.
image_context: From the end of the 19th century to the present, the relationship between brain white matter fiber structure and language has been perpetually explored, and the technological advancements are streamlined daily. Among these advancements, diffusion-weighted magnetic resonance imaging, also known as DTI, allowed tracking of white matter fiber structure in vivo (Janssen et al., 2023). The post-processing techniques of such sequence are mainly divided into deterministic and probabilistic fiber tracking. In the deterministic fiber tracking technology, through neuroanatomy, a variety of white matter fiber structures are confirmed; thus, it has a high degree of reliability and restoration authenticity. For example, the arcuate bundle, which is the highlight of this review, is difficult to display via conventional MRI techniques, let alone the substructure, while DTI sequences can provide very clear morphological structures such as that in <xref rid="fnins-17-1307834-g002" ref-type="fig">Figure 2</xref>. After restoration of this brain structure noninvasively, as a clinician, a question is posed: how can it be used and researched? Thus far, we found that this technology can be utilized more in stroke and brain tumor. For brain tumors, it can be precisely positioned prior to surgery to significantly improve the quality of life of patients after surgery, and secondly, it can be used to predict neurological function recovery, such as language and cognition after surgery (. After restoration of this brain structure noninvasively, as a clinician, a question is posed: how can it be used and researched? Thus far, we found that this technology can be utilized more in stroke and brain tumor. For brain tumors, it can be precisely positioned prior to surgery to significantly improve the quality of life of patients after surgery, and secondly, it can be used to predict neurological function recovery, such as language and cognition after surgery (Mori and van Zijl, 2002; Yang et al., 2017; Zimmermann et al., 2020). For stroke, the highlight is on predicting the recovery of language ability and exploring new methods of diagnosis and treatment, such as neuromodulation therapy (Puig et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2020).
